turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments first this morning, in Number 79-1344; Michael M. against the Superior Court of Sonoma County, California. Now, Mr. Jilka, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: My name is Gregory Jilka. I am one of the attorneys this Court seeking reversal of the judgment of the Supreme Court of California, a judgment which upheld the validity, the perpetrator, where the female is under the age of 18 years." The operative facts in this case are simply that Michael stands accused of the crime. He has not been convicted of the crime and comes before this Court clothed with the presumption of innocence. He is currently awaiting trial, pending the decision of the Court in this case. Both California and the United States, as amicus on behalf of California, have suggested to this Court that Michael is guilty of a crime of which he is not charged; that crime is case is unsupported by any judicial finding and is immaterial to the issues presented. And, in my opinion, tends to obfus­ cate the issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the charge? I mean, could the charge possibly embrace forcible as well as statutory?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: No, Justice Rehnquist, it could not. is the constitutional validity of the statute on its face --

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- is that correct? So it's equally irrelevant that Michael was 17 years old?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He was not -- I mean, if you're right on the first --

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- the second follows.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: I do believe, however, that we have client are, perhaps, relevant. But if the issue is the constitutional validity of this statute on its face, then neither the alleged facts of this episode, nor the age of the Defendant, are really rele­ vant, are they?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, as far as the facial issue of constitutionality goes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Counsel, could I ask you a question about the California situation? Could he have been charged -- well, was he charged with a felony? felony.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could he have been charged for a misde­ meanor?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, this statute could have been charged as a misdemeanor in the discretion of the district attorney, the prosecuting authority. against this man?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, don't we have to take as true, for purposes of decision, whatever facts are alleged therein? Assuming that --

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that's a fair statement. QUESTION -- they will prove those and that they will not have to prove anything not alleged therein? that's presented. Ordinarily, if a motion to dismiss and information is denied, the case goes to trial. And we have

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but there -- there is one thing under 17, when it didn’t do it for people over 17?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's -- that certainly could be made, Your Honor. I think that that would be a much different case because the discrimination --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would be a different case, but it would be a constitutional challenge, wouldn't it?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: It could be, it certainly could be. we feel, the traditional stereotypes. Because

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: California, if it tries to protect females, must protect males also?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Similarly situated males, yes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That the constitution requires it to do so?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that is our contention.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then, that would be true of forcible rape

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can women be guilty of the offense under this California statute as aiders and abettors?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, that's correct. As a racial minority --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Accessories?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, that's right, that's right, they could, certainly, but not as a principal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can the state -- can a state constitu­ tionally make it a criminal act for a woman of, let us say, 30, to seduce a young boy of 14 or 15? the realm of the state's legitimate concern --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Most states do, do they not?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, in one form or another; for example, in California, it is a very minor offense, it's contributing to the delinquency of a minor, it's a misdemeanor. Whereas original jurisdiction is predicated in the statute and I just don't know if the California Supreme Court would have taken this case had it been in a lower level court; that's problematic.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, well, the penalties that are prescribed are the same for aiders and abettors as for the principals, certainly, if thdt's your question, yes. I would point out to the case, a new Eighth Circuit opinion in a case called Navedo v. Preisser, which was de­ cided on September 22nd of this year, in which the Eighth Circuit invalidated the Iowa statutory rape law. That was a different statutory rape law than California, certainly, but

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of this year.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Jilka, excuse me again for inter­ rupting. Mr. Justice Stewart made the point about aiders and abettors and you said they are equally culpable and equally punishable. Doesn't that entirely destroy your case?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well because, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the discrimination if both the male and the female can be punished equally and there can't be a crime unless they are both liable for some kind of punish­ ment?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well a male and a female can be punished equally only if the female aids and abets the male in perform­ ing an act of sexual intercourse with a female under the age of 18. There is no -- what we're focusing on here should be the conduct. If a male engages in sexual intercourse with a

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's certainly correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you're not challenging that?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it's voluntary, then don't you, by hypothesis, have an aiding and abetting situation?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: I suppose so, Your Honor. However, I would represent to the Court that in practice, that the victim, the purported victim is never charged with a crime. I couldn't -- a male and a female is basically the same act, a mutual act; for a male under the same circumstances it's a felony, but for the female to participate, it is not. And I do not believe that a female can and would be charged as an aider and abettor, a female so-called victim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is a case in the federal system that said that the so-called victim in a Mann Act prosecution, could be guilty of conspiracy of her own -- to carrying on her own transportation in interstate commerce for immoral purposes; wouldn't the same principal apply? Your Honor, and I -- in California, Your Honor -- and I don't have the case authority for that, I certainly wish I did. But I believe that to be the case, that the female is in practice and in law not considered to be culpable as an aider and abettor. That is the female victim, excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would your case still survive if Cali­ fornia had a statute that, contributing to the delinquency of treatment of the sexes, but that is not the case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the range of sentences were the same for both the present statute and the one I hypothesized would there by any difference?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: If the sentences were the same and the substantive elements of the crime were the same, I think that we would have great difficulty with an equal protection tack -- I agree. I would point out to the Court that the California case law resoundingly demonstrates that the motivation for the enactment of the statute is the concern for the virtue of young girls and not the pregnancy prevention rationale. I would point out the cases cited at pages 25 and 26 of the brief of the Petitioner. These cases span the range of years 1895 through 1975 --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, Your Supreme Court has said otherwise recently, hasn't it?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: The Supreme Court has recently said otherwise in this case, but --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even when the Supreme Court of California pronounces what the purpose of their statute was?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. That is correct. This Court has the duty to look into the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well how could we know any better than the Supreme Court of California or the California legislature what the purpose of enacting a statute was?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well in answer to that, I would say that the burden of demonstrating the purpose of the statute is on the government in this case and that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean the presumption of constitu­ tionality doesn't attend to the statutes in California?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: There is a -- certainly a presumption of constitutionality, Your Honor, but I believe under the government to justify the classifications of the statute. I believe that that was the reasoning of the Ninth Circuit adopted in the case of the United States v. Hicks, which was cited in our brief, a recent Ninth Circuit case which invalidated a federal statute which --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you know, has the government taken appeal on that one?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: It's my information that the government has filed a petition for a writ of certiorari; I'm sorry, I

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well that's certainly part of it, Your Honor. But the pregnancy prevention rationale as pointed out in the Meloon case out of the First Circuit, is suspect, time was spent by both you and your brother, in your briefs, in talking about the purpose but isn't it the duty of this Court to look at the statute on its face and to, depending upon what level of so-called scrutiny one wants to apply, to uphold the statute if a rational basis can be conceived for its existence?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Well, Your Honor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it a pretty elusive quest to try to find out what the purpose of the California legislature was, back at the time of the enactment of this statute, statute had a variety of purposes? the classifications served are substantially related to those purposes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any legislative history of this state legislation that one can look at?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Essentially none.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I didn’t think so.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: There really is none. All we have is the California Court opinions, basically.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: In this case, certainly; and as between males and females of all ages who essentially engage in the same kind of conduct, be it reprehensible or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But as I understood your answer to one of my earlier questions, you don't attack this statute on the basis that it protects people only under 17 years old?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: No, that's correct. That is correct. If it please the Court, I'd like to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It punishes males of any age, does it not?

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well do you think you are fatally wounded if we accept the purpose of the statute as announced by the State Supreme Court in this case? Justice Mosk didn't seem to think so.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't think that we would be fatally wounded. I think that it would be impossible this case, sex, the male gender is essentially used as a proxy for culpability, and we contend that that's an imperfect proxy. There is a weak congruence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, assume there is an equal culpability, but unequal impact on the parties engaging in this conduct; which is what, I take it, the California Court relied upon, and that apparently drawing the conclusion that it is permissible to punish the male even though the female is equally culpable, that's all you're saying.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: I'm saying she is equally culpable because she can certainly appreciate the dangers and risks of a decision to engage in sexual intercourse. Just as much as the male. There is no showing that punishing the female for example, or punishing the male instead of the female, is

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the State is saying to'" the male, legislature to take into consideration -- that the legislature has taken into consideration possible risks as well as actual consequences.

turns.name: JILKA
turns.text: Yes, that is certainly true.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler. MR, KRIEGLER: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and which do not reflect the type of invidious discrimination which the Equal Protection Clause prohibits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler, do prosecutors in California always press these as felonies?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, where is the line of distinction? Here you have two young people about the same age, one year and 18 days apart, and you press it as a felony?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe it was pressed as a felony in this case due to the particular facts involved. And these particular facts are revealed in the appendix --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I read the appendix thoroughly and I would say the particular facts would seem to cut the other way.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well Your Honor, the prosecutor obviously disagreed based upon the fact that the victim clearly testified, under oath at the preliminary hearing, that she submitted to the act of intercourse only after being slugged was not even as a felony, it was under our Juvenile Court law under which there are no felonies or misdemeanors; it is not a criminal branch of our judiciary. served by Penal Code Section 261.5 are the deterrence of teen­ age pregnancy, the prevention of physical injury to minor females from sexual intercourse.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler, would either of those purposes be served any less well by a statute that was entirely neutral that applied to the female as well as the male?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor, particularly in this case. If our statute were gender neutral in this case, the victim, Sharon, would be, at least on the face of the statute, subject to prosecution. If she were subject to pro­ secution, first of ail, she would be less likely to report the the --

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I would just point out that in view of the testimony that she gave under oath, she was not a willing participant in this act; she submitted --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, who bought the liquor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you say she was not a willing par­ ticipant --

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: She was not a willing participant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well doesn't the Appendix show that she began the intimacies?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Your Honor, she engaged in a certain amount of kissing, apparently, from the record, with the Defendant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Making out, the term was used, and objected to and sustained.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe that was Mr. Jilka's term that he used in the preliminary hearing. However, it's clear that she did not wish to go further and proceed to an act of intercourse. And indeed, in that act she bore all the risks of the potential pregnancy, she was not willing to engage in all, they bear only on the decision of the prosecutor as to whether to prosecute on a felony or a misdemeanor, isn't that so? And that issue is not before us. Only the constitution­ ality, as Justice Stewart has said several times, on its face, is the issue here.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: We agree. And our only point would be'that insofar as Petitioner is pursuing the argument that the statute is unconstitutional as applied to him as a 17-year old, he cannot have it both ways; he cannot straddle the fence and say it's unconstitutional to apply this to me as a minor, on the other hand ignoring the specific conduct that he engaged in --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why not? It's perfectly apparent on the face of the statute that it would apply to a consensual act between 17-year olds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why is it important if you're defending the statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do we know that? You're just --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- instead of the prosecution?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and there's been no trial and no finding of fact, so we don't know whether that's true or not.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, there has been no trial. However, whether the parties are engaged in the type of conduct that occurred in this case or as Your Honor suggests, whether we view the statute on its face, we submit that the statute is nonetheless constitutional because it serves these two important governmental interests that I have mentioned and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say that the reason it serves it better than a sex neutral statute is that after the act has been committed, then there's a greater likelihood that it will be reported by the female?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Right -- Females have

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't think the female should ever 1 be punished for engaging in this kind of conduct as a deter­ rent to further examples of the same kind of conduct?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well I think the state might -- the state legislature might, in the exercise of its sound judg­ ment, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would. it be rational for a parent to say it's perfectly all right for my sons to do this, but it's wrong for my daughters to do this and therefore I will punish

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: It would depend upon the circumstances Your Honor, and the act --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well it's always a risk-creating act, isn't it?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: It is, but the risks are borne uniquely by the female. But it might --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it takes two participants every time of these extraordinary risks which Petitioner has conceded in it's brief, to be compelling state interests, the legislature has reasonably concluded, we believe, that the sexes are not similarly situated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Has the legislature reasonably concluded that those risks have been sufficient to deter females from voluntarily engaging in this kind of conduct?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, the question of deterrence is one that is very difficult to answer; we don't know how many people have or have not been deterred by the existence of this

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not very many have.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The point is, must be, I think, that the risk of pregnancy is a deterrence with respect to the female, and --

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: I submit--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and in order to -- and there is no such deterrence with respect to the male. And in order to deter the male this statute is necessary, applicable to males

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it applied to girls 10 years old, right?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any literature that shows that there was a rash of pregnancies among 9 year olds in 1850?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: No. We are quite confident that the statute as originally enacted in 1950 was intended to prevent physical injury to minor females, 9 year olds certainly, at that time --

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well the statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I'm going to ask for a citation when you answer.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Fine. The statute, Mr. Justice Marshall, was amended incrementally, at three stages: the age of the protection for the female raised to 14, 16, and eventually 18, in 1913. We believe that while there is no concrete legislative history to support the reasoning for those increases, certainly one inference that could reasonably be drawn as the age of the victim increased the possibility of pregnancy occurring was more frequent. Now -- points I would like to make in regard to the question of legislative intent, if I might?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: In 1967, California passed its Thera­ peutic Abortion Act, and that is cited in our brief. And that of course was well prior to the time that this Court enunciated the rule that abortions were constitutionally man­ dated -- must be made available. In that act, the one category of victim that was specifically named as being unconditionally eligible for abortion was statutory rape victims under our former statutory rape law; California Penal Code Section 261 Subdivision 1. Because that particular category of victim under Section 261 Subdivision 1, was specifically singled out as being eligible for abortion, we believe that it's very clear at least at that it was called unlawful sexual intercourse. We believe that since that occurred three years after the legislative defer-

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: -- no chastity requirement. Well, there's no chastity requirement in our statute, number one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why then, all these statistics on the great rise in pregnancies of unmarried teenagers, 12-13-14 is not constitutionally required to treat dissimilarly situ­ ated sexes the same way. We have established that the very narrow category of victim in this case is in need of special protection. Minor males are not in need of the same protec­ tion. That is not to say, however, that minor males do not get protection under the California laws.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that because there's a greater moral stigma to this act if it's performed by an unmarried female than by an unmarried male?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: I don't think it is necessarily a moral stigma, it may be a basis of -- of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does California, is it the policy of the state of California to place greater importance on the chastity of the female than on the chastity of the male?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: I don't think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In moral terms?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or you rely entirely on pregnancy --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: California legislature determined to protect the chastity of minor females, not of all people of all ages, or of both sexes, and your opponent's argument is that California is constitutionally required, if it protects minor females to at least protect minor males. We don't have here the question of whether it also is constitutionally required to protect the chastity of males and females of all ages but that would be the next case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You certainly must argue -- you certainly the consequences socially, economically and in various other ways, are much greater on the girl, is that not so?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is the entire basis of our has, thankfully, pointed out -- the consequences of these pregnancies are just disastrous on minor females. Eight out should be protected under the statute, that creates another problem in California, because she, as an accomplice or an aider or abettor, must have her testimony corroborated under California Penal Code Section 111.1. And under that section, her testimony standing by itself won't be sufficient to con­ stitute substantial evidence for a conviction under California law. These acts are, by and large, committed in privacy. This would be a very substantial problem for the state of statute. When we add to that the fact that in the great majority of these cases, the perpetrator is the adult -- is an adult. We have cited statistics, in our brief which show that it's a very rare case indeed that a minor male is pro­ secuted. Perhaps only one --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But in terms of the actual conduct you follows.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's your argument about the female; she suffers a greater risk of harm, and therefore, she should not be deterred. She should know intelligently, no matter how young she is, that she should not do this.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, this statute, Your Honor, is consistent with all of the California laws which prohibit sexual offenses with minors, whether they be male or females. Under California law where the minor is protected from acts involving lewd or lascivious conduct, or annoying or molesting whether the minor who engages in those acts with the adult counterpart is acting consensually or not. The statute is there for the protection of that particular individual, because of the consequences of the act. And it doesn't matter whether the act is consensual or not; the severe consequences to the minor victim in all of our California statutes, are recognized, crime that's committed. participant never suffers any adverse consequences other than legal prohibition?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: We are unaware of any substantial adverse consequences to the minor male.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's financial responsibility, some­ times. There's physical harm, sometimes, isn't there?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: We are unaware of any --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well how about venereal disease?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is a potential harm that both suffer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To either one?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about an irate father?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: That may be a more practical consid­ eration, I don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And some liability for support, even­ liquor in this case, Your Honor. I don't think she plied anybody with liquor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well she -- she drank it herself?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, she did, certainly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if he'd given it to her, he'd have been the aggressor?

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, we don't -- we don't base our --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I hope not.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: -- on that fact, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I come back again to where we've as applied, but on its face, and nothing else.

turns.name: KRIEGLER
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor. We would strongly urge this Court to recognize the severe consequences that befall only the teenage female participant in an act of inter­ course, regardless of the age of the male participant. And with the Court being aware, as it is, of the consequences of teenage pregnancy, I don't need to remind the Court certainly of the statements made in earlier cases. I know, Mr. Justice Powell speaking for the Court in Bellotti v. Baird certainly females in the state of California. The problem is conceded by Petitioner to be one of monumental proportions. Superior Court of Sonoma County and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court.
